system_prompt:
You are distilling one chapter of a guitar method book into a short summary.

The user will give you a chapter file with topic-organized verbatim quotes
plus the human-rationale notes for each. Write a one-paragraph summary (4-7
sentences) that captures:

  - what this chapter teaches (the techniques, concepts, rules, examples)
  - the master's characteristic moves visible in this chapter (if any)
  - the load-bearing terminology this chapter introduces

Reference the topic labels from the chapter file when useful. Do not invent
information not present in the chapter. If the chapter is biographical or
introductory rather than instructional, say so explicitly.

Output just the paragraph — no preamble, no JSON, no markdown formatting.
user_prompt:
Chapter 11: III7 VI7 II7 V7

<<<CHAPTER FILE
# Chapter 11 — III7 VI7 II7 V7

_Pages 78-105._

> **Verbatim-quote constraint.** The quotes below are exact substrings of the raw transcript produced by `pdftotext`. The transcript may contain extraction artifacts where the book's typeface confuses the tool — e.g. `7irst` or `<irst` for `first`, `speci7ic` for `specific`. **Do NOT manually fix these in this file**: the quote-fidelity validator compares against the raw transcript, so a 'fixed' quote will fail verification and break Stage 2 idempotency. If a future pipeline iteration adds artifact-normalization, it must normalize the transcript and these files together.

## III7 VI7 II7 V7 progression intro

> The last harmonic formula we will deal with in this book is III7 VI7 117 V7. There are many different examples |
> given because there are many different attractive sounding chords that fit together weil on this progression. These |
> examples are organized, once again, according to which chord starts the progression.

**Significance (p. 78).** Establishes chapter subject and organizational principle.

## Substituting iii7/vi7 for III7/VI7

> Occasionally, you may wish to try using iii7 types in place of IIJ7 types, or vi7 types in place of V17 types. In
> many cases, either of these chords would sound very good — experiment and see what you think. Also, you might fool
>
> around with IF or 17 for II]? {these are all common sounds),

**Significance (p. 78).** Concrete substitution rules expanding harmonic vocabulary.

## Altered dominant definition

> almost all of the examples use altered dominant 7th
> types (altered dominants are those with #9’s, 69’s, #5°s, and 65’s), which are very “spicy” chords.

**Significance (p. 78).** Explicit definition of altered dominants by enumeration.

## Pacing altered-chord listening

> Take this
> whole section in small doses and add some of your favorite examples of these IIJ7, VI7 LI7 ¥7’s to some of the more
> “normal” progressions you have already studied; the ear will accept altered chords more easily in most cases if they are
> prepared with sounds of a more normal, diatonic “major scaley’’ nature first.

**Significance (p. 78).** Ear-training rule: prepare altered with diatonic.

## Combining consonant and dissonant progressions

> This type of technique (that is, of combining the consonant and dissonant types of progressions) is also fun for
> your ear because of the contrast; and it is good for you because it will get you into the spirit of combining shorter
> ‘progressions in order to make longer progressions.

**Significance (p. 79).** Compositional principle behind mixing diatonic and altered progressions.

## Resolve V7 to I reminder

> Don’t forget to occasionally try resolving the V7’s to I’s.

**Significance (p. 79).** V7-I resolution as always-available option.

## Chord substitution note (A7b5 → D7b9b5)

> TRY THIS CHORD IN PLACE OF A7b5 WHENEVER A7b5 PROGRESSES TO D7b9b5.

**Significance (p. 82).** Contextual substitution tied to specific motion.

## Book must be studied in order

> If you decide to skip around, and you run across a chord that you cannot find the right fingering for, the chances
> are great that the fingering was given earlier somewhere. Please remember, for your own good, this book is meant to be
>
> approached in order, from the beginning to the end.

**Significance (p. 78).** Sequencing requirement: fingerings taught cumulatively.

CHAPTER FILE>>>
